content:
```json
{
  "mechanism_genome": "Develop a synthetic-to-real transfer framework for plant disease detection that first trains a robust CNN on PlantVillage-style controlled leaf images, then synthesizes field-realistic variations of those images using domain randomization and GAN-based augmentation, and finally fine-tunes the model on a small held-out field dataset. The method should include a multi-disease loss function, occlusion-aware attention, and lighting/pose invariant features.",
  "niche_genome": "Automated plant disease detection for agricultural fields, especially scenarios where crops are imaged under variable lighting, angles, backgrounds, occlusions, and multiple disease co-occurrence.",
  "observation_genome": "The proposed method is expected to outperform models trained only on PlantVillage on field images, while requiring only a small field dataset for fine-tuning. It should improve robustness to lighting, angle, and background changes and generalize better to multi-disease field scenes.",
  "limitation_genome": "The method still depends on access to a small field dataset for fine-tuning, and synthetic augmentation cannot fully capture all real-world variability such as extreme weather damage, insect damage, or non-leaf disease symptoms.",
  "delta_genome": "Compared with prior work, this idea explicitly couples a large controlled benchmark with synthetic field-realistic data and field fine-tuning, rather than treating controlled benchmark accuracy as sufficient for deployment.",
  "claim_genome": "A plant disease detection system trained on controlled images and then adapted to field conditions using synthetic-to-real transfer will outperform a benchmark-only model on field images with complex backgrounds, occlusion, and multiple diseases."
}
```
prompt:
You are a senior researcher in Computer Vision / Plant Pathology who understands how scientific ideas evolve.
Below is an important research lineage in this area, where each paper evolves from its predecessors:

**[2016] Deep Neural Networks Based Recognition of Plant Diseases by Leaf Image Classification**
Key Contribution: Demonstrated that deep CNNs trained on leaf images can automatically recognize 13 plant disease types, establishing the feasibility of DL-based plant disease classification.
Target Problem: Manual visual inspection of plant diseases is slow, subjective, and requires expert knowledge that is unavailable in many agricultural regions.
Core Method: Fine-tune a deep CNN (CaffeNet architecture) on augmented leaf image datasets; use transfer learning from ImageNet to overcome limited agricultural training data.
Observed Evidence: Achieved 96.3% average accuracy across 13 disease classes; showed that deep architectures outperform handcrafted feature methods (color histograms, SIFT).
Remaining Limitation: Trained and tested on controlled single-leaf images with uniform backgrounds; no evaluation on field images with complex backgrounds, occlusion, or multiple diseases.
Expected Result/Claim: Demonstrated that deep CNNs trained on leaf images can automatically recognize 13 plant disease types, establishing the feasibility of DL-based plant disease classification.

  ↓

**[2016] Using Deep Learning for Image-Based Plant Disease Detection**
Key Contribution: Introduced the PlantVillage benchmark dataset of 54,306 images across 26 diseases and demonstrated 99.35% classification accuracy using GoogLeNet and AlexNet architectures.
Target Problem: No large-scale standardized benchmark exists for plant disease detection; prior works use small private datasets making comparison impossible.
Core Method: Curate a public dataset (PlantVillage) of 54,306 controlled images spanning 14 crop species and 26 diseases; train GoogLeNet and AlexNet with transfer learning from ImageNet.
Observed Evidence: GoogLeNet achieves 99.35% top-1 accuracy on held-out test set; transfer learning dramatically reduces data requirements compared to training from scratch.
Remaining Limitation: Dataset contains only lab-controlled images with homogeneous backgrounds; models trained on PlantVillage fail catastrophically on real-world field images with varying lighting, angles, and backgrounds.
Expected Result/Claim: Introduced the PlantVillage benchmark dataset of 54,306 images across 26 diseases and demonstrated 99.35% classification accuracy using GoogLeNet and AlexNet architectures.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: Deep Neural Networks Based Recognition of Plant Disea...)
  Change Summary: Scales up the same CNN-based approach with a much larger public benchmark dataset (PlantVillage); same architectural paradigm (transfer learning from ImageNet) but establishes community-wide reproducible evaluation.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work changed; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source changed; new work changed; Expected Result/Claim: source changed; new work changed
  Field Change Summary: local change from the source driven by method; changed method, target problem, limitation, claim; not reused observation; added target problem, observation.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=Fine-tune a deep CNN (CaffeNet architecture) on augmented leaf image datasets; use transfer learning from ImageNet to overcome limited agricultural training data. | new=Curate a public dataset (PlantVillage) of 54,306 controlled images spanning 14 crop species and 26 diseases; train GoogLeNet and AlexNet with transfer learning from ImageNet.

  ↓

**[2020] PlantDoc: A Dataset for Visual Plant Disease Detection**
Key Contribution: Created the first real-world plant disease dataset (PlantDoc) with internet-scraped images under uncontrolled conditions, exposing the lab-to-field generalization gap.
Target Problem: Models trained on PlantVillage (lab images) suffer dramatic accuracy drops when deployed in real agricultural settings with diverse backgrounds and imaging conditions.
Core Method: Curate 2,598 images from the internet spanning 13 plant species and 17 disease classes under real-world conditions; benchmark with VGG16, InceptionV3, and InceptionResNetV2.
Observed Evidence: Models trained jointly on PlantVillage + PlantDoc improve real-world accuracy by up to 31%; shows significant domain shift between lab and field images.
Remaining Limitation: Dataset is still relatively small (2,598 images); does not include detection/localization annotations; limited to single disease per image without severity assessment.
Expected Result/Claim: Created the first real-world plant disease dataset (PlantDoc) with internet-scraped images under uncontrolled conditions, exposing the lab-to-field generalization gap.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: Using Deep Learning for Image-Based Plant Disease Det...)
  Change Summary: Directly challenges PlantVillage's lab-controlled setting by creating a competing real-world benchmark; exposes the generalization failure and redefines what success means for the field.
  Main Changed Field: Target Problem
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work changed; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: parallel route for the same target problem driven by target problem; changed method, target problem; not reused observation, limitation, claim; added target problem, observation, limitation, claim.
  Main Change: Target Problem | source=No large-scale standardized benchmark exists for plant disease detection; prior works use small private datasets making comparison impossible. | new=Models trained on PlantVillage (lab images) suffer dramatic accuracy drops when deployed in real agricultural settings with diverse backgrounds and imaging conditions.

  ↓

**[2023] Explainable Vision Transformer Enabled Convolutional Neural Network for Plant Disease Identification: PlantXViT**
Key Contribution: Combined Vision Transformer self-attention with CNN local features in a lightweight 0.8M parameter model achieving strong accuracy on field images with Grad-CAM explainability.
Target Problem: Pure CNNs capture local textures but miss global context of disease patterns; existing ViT models are too large for IoT-based agricultural edge deployment.
Core Method: Hybrid architecture: initial VGG16 blocks for local feature extraction, followed by an inception block and 4 transformer encoder stacks for global attention; only 0.8M parameters.
Observed Evidence: Achieves 98.33% on Rice, 93.55% on Apple, 92.59% on Maize datasets under field conditions; outperforms 5 SOTA methods; Grad-CAM validates focus on disease regions.
Remaining Limitation: Evaluated on single-crop datasets independently; no cross-crop transfer learning explored; explainability is post-hoc and does not provide textual disease descriptions.
Expected Result/Claim: Combined Vision Transformer self-attention with CNN local features in a lightweight 0.8M parameter model achieving strong accuracy on field images with Grad-CAM explainability.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: PlantDoc: A Dataset for Visual Plant Disease Detection)
  Change Summary: Combines CNN local feature extraction (from early CNN papers) with Vision Transformer global attention mechanisms; hybrid architecture addresses both the texture sensitivity and global context requirements exposed by field-condition datasets.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source changed; new work changed; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: combining multiple source ideas driven by method; changed method, target problem, limitation; not reused observation, claim; added method, target problem, observation, claim.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=Curate 2,598 images from the internet spanning 13 plant species and 17 disease classes under real-world conditions; benchmark with VGG16, InceptionV3, and InceptionResNetV2. | new=Hybrid architecture: initial VGG16 blocks for local feature extraction, followed by an inception block and 4 transformer encoder stacks for global attention; only 0.8M parameters.

  ↓

**[2025] Plant Disease Classification in the Wild Using Vision Transformers and Mixture of Experts**
Key Contribution: Introduced Mixture-of-Experts architecture with ViT backbone for robust plant disease classification across both controlled and in-the-wild conditions, achieving strong generalization.
Target Problem: Single-expert models trained on controlled datasets fail to generalize to diverse real-world conditions; the distribution shift between lab and field remains the critical bottleneck.
Core Method: ViT backbone extracts patch-level features which are normalized and routed through a Mixture-of-Experts module where a gating network assigns weights to multiple expert classifiers, each specializing in different visual conditions.
Observed Evidence: Achieves SOTA on both PlantVillage (controlled) and PlantDoc (wild) simultaneously; MoE routing learns to activate different experts for lab vs. field conditions.
Remaining Limitation: Still relies on pre-defined disease categories; cannot identify novel diseases or provide natural language explanations; limited to image-only inputs without environmental context.
Expected Result/Claim: Introduced Mixture-of-Experts architecture with ViT backbone for robust plant disease classification across both controlled and in-the-wild conditions, achieving strong generalization.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: Explainable Vision Transformer Enabled Convolutional...)
  Change Summary: Extends the ViT-CNN hybrid paradigm into a multi-expert system where specialized sub-networks handle different visual conditions; diversifies the model capacity to cover both controlled and wild domains simultaneously.
  Main Changed Field: Target Problem
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source changed; new work changed; Remaining Limitation: source not reused; new work newly added; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: reuse in a shifted target problem driven by target problem; changed method, target problem, observation; not reused limitation, claim; added method, target problem, limitation, claim.
  Main Change: Target Problem | source=Pure CNNs capture local textures but miss global context of disease patterns; existing ViT models are too large for IoT-based agricultural edge deployment. | new=Single-expert models trained on controlled datasets fail to generalize to diverse real-world conditions; the distribution shift between lab and field remains the critical bottle...

  ↓

**[2025] A Vision-Language Foundation Model for Leaf Disease Identification**
Key Contribution: Proposed SCOLD, a vision-language foundation model pre-trained on 186K image-caption pairs aligned with 97 plant disease concepts, enabling zero-shot and few-shot disease identification with textual explanations.
Target Problem: Existing plant disease models are closed-set classifiers that cannot handle novel diseases, provide textual explanations, or leverage symptom descriptions for reasoning.
Core Method: Pre-train a vision-language model (SCOLD) using soft-target contrastive learning on 186K plant leaf image-caption pairs spanning 97 disease concepts; enable zero-shot classification via text-image alignment.
Observed Evidence: Outperforms OpenAI CLIP-L, BioCLIP, and SigLIP2 on zero-shot classification, few-shot learning, and image-text retrieval benchmarks for plant disease identification.
Remaining Limitation: Limited to leaf-level disease identification; does not integrate temporal progression, environmental factors, or management recommendations; caption quality depends on curation.
Expected Result/Claim: Proposed SCOLD, a vision-language foundation model pre-trained on 186K image-caption pairs aligned with 97 plant disease concepts, enabling zero-shot and few-shot disease identification with textual explanations.

  ^ **[Source-to-new-work change note]** (from: Plant Disease Classification in the Wild Using Vision...)
  Change Summary: Fundamentally new paradigm shift from closed-set classifiers to open-vocabulary vision-language foundation models; replaces discrete class labels with continuous semantic space enabling zero-shot generalization to unseen diseases.
  Main Changed Field: Core Method
  Field Changes: Target Problem: source changed; new work newly added; Core Method: source changed; new work newly added; Observed Evidence: source not reused; new work newly added; Remaining Limitation: source changed; new work changed; Expected Result/Claim: source not reused; new work newly added
  Field Change Summary: main-method replacement driven by method; changed method, target problem, limitation; not reused observation, claim; added method, target problem, observation, claim.
  Main Change: Core Method | source=ViT backbone extracts patch-level features which are normalized and routed through a Mixture-of-Experts module where a gating network assigns weights to multiple expert classifi... | new=Pre-train a vision-language model (SCOLD) using soft-target contrastive learning on 186K plant leaf image-caption pairs spanning 97 disease concepts; enable zero-shot classifica...


The key unresolved question at the frontier of this lineage:
SCOLD enables zero-shot disease identification but operates on single static leaf images without temporal, environmental, or treatment context. How can vision-language foundation models be extended to incorporate multi-temporal progression data, environmental sensors, and treatment outcome feedback for interactive disease management advisory systems?

Based on the lineage history, source-to-new-work changes, and the current frontier question, generate a new research idea as the next evolutionary node.

Return ONLY valid JSON. Do not wrap it in Markdown fences.
{
  "idea": "<short name of the proposed idea, 5-15 words>",
  "idea_description": "<150-250 words describing the full research idea>",
  "technical_plan": {
    "target_problem": "<specific problem, dataset, population, or phenomenon>",
    "core_method": "<technical method or mechanism, not a vague goal>",
    "limitation_addressed": "<frontier limitation repaired by this idea>",

  },
  "lineage_connection": {
    "source_ideas": ["<paper title or source idea used by this proposal>", "..."],
    "reused_parts": ["<specific method, evidence, data, or problem framing reused from the sources>", "..."],
    "changed_parts": ["<specific modified, replaced, combined, or newly added part>", "..."],
    "why_next_step": "<why this idea follows from the provided context>",
    "lineage_risk": "<most likely way this source connection could be wrong>"
  },
  "evaluation_plan": {
    "setup": "<concrete experiment, benchmark, proof, or analysis setup>",
    "baselines": ["<baseline>", "..."],
    "metrics": ["<metric>", "..."],
    "risks": ["<risk>", "..."]
  }
}
